1.definition: common title for a collection of resources. NOTE: Title identifies elements of a series collectively, combined with information about what volumes are available at the source cite.

2.definition: reference from one resource to another - TEST

3.definition: associated through a common heritage such as produced to a common product specification

4.definition: inverse of wasGeneratedBy where the domain resource generated the range resource implying that the domain resource is a temporal event, such as a data processing action, and the range resource is an entity

5.definition: inverse of wasDerivedFrom where the range resource was derived from the domain resource

7.definition: reference to resources that are parts of this resource

8.definition: reference to a master resource of which this one is a part

9.definition: inverse of operatesOn where the domain resource is, or can be, operated on (i.e.queried) by the range resource which must be an agent (i.e. is something that bears some form of responsibility for the operation taking place)

10.definition: an equivalent relationship to srv:operatesOn from a Service to a Dataset

11.definition: part of same structured set of data held in a computer

12.definition: resource is a revision of associated resource

13.definition: associated through a common heritage such as produced to a common product specification

14.definition: part of a set of imagery that when used together, provides three-dimensional images

15.definition: the domain resource was derived from the range resource which must be an entity (i.e. not an actor with agency or a temporal event)

16.definition: the domain resource was generated by the range resource which must have been a temporal event such as a data processing action or a field survey

17.definition: The domain resource was informed by the range resource which must have been an Entity (i.e. not an Agent with agency or an Activity temporal event). This is a looser association than wasDerivedFrom and does not indicate direct derivation, as wasDerivedFrom does.

18.definition: Examples include vibrating truck and explosives.
18.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

19.definition: Clearly defined administrative areas of a political, natural, or regulatory boundary nature, excluding scientific speculative boundaries such as geological boundaries, ecological / ecosystem boundaries etc.  Examples include coastlines (because coastlines define certain administrative boundaries), state boundaries, electoral boundaries, mineral tenement boundaries, petroleum title boundaries and marine protected areas.
19.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

20.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

21.definition: Photographs of a part of the earth's surface taken by a camera mounted in an aircraft for mapping purposes. This usually consists of a series of overlapping vertical photos taken in strips which can form the basis for mapping.
21.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

22.definition: Pertains to both marine and freshwater ecosystems. For example, reefs
22.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

23.definition: Data describing aquifer properties. Porous, water-bearing layers of sand, gravel, and rock below the earth's surface; reservoirs for groundwater.
23.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

24.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

25.definition: Includes sub-surface distribution of metamorphic, sedimentary and igneous rocks and interpreted linear structural features such as folds, faults, lineaments and shearzones
25.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

26.definition: Measurements of ocean depth and the charting of the topography of the ocean floor.
26.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

27.definition: The benthic zone is the ecological region at the lowest level of a body of water such as an ocean or a lake, including the sediment surface and some sub-surface layers.
27.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

28.definition: Stratigraphy based on the paleontologic aspects of rocks.
28.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

29.definition: A borehole is any narrow shaft drilled in the ground, either vertically or horizontally, it includes Petroleum Wells, Stratigraphic Holes, Mineral Holes, Geothermal Holes, Water Bores
29.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

30.definition: A public record of the extent, value, and ownership of land within a district for purposes of taxation.
30.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

31.definition: The use of non-radiogenic dating studies such as U-Pb ratios
31.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

32.definition: A Coal Seam Methane hole is drilled specifically to discover, asses or produce from a coal seam methane reservoir. EXAMPLE UPDATE.
32.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

33.definition: AEM, ground EM, Point located data, grids, derivative grids, algorithms, images, sections, volumes
33.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

34.definition: The density of a material is defined as its mass per unit volume.
34.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

35.definition: Dissolved solids" refer to any minerals, salts, metals, cations or anions dissolved in water.
35.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

36.definition: Satellite and air platform imagery. This includes imagery designed to represent surficial condition of the earth such as Landsat, SPOT, MODIS, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography. Remotely sensed data acquired from earth observation satellites and Sensing Instruments for both scientific and operational applications, such as LANDSAT 5,LANDSAT 7,RADARSAT 1 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR), ERS 2 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR) TERRA Modis,AQUA Modis, NOAA 17, SPOT, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography
36.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

37.definition: An earthquake is the shaking and vibration at the surface of the earth caused by underground movement along a fault plane or by volcanic activity.
37.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

38.definition: Elevation data such as Digital Elevation Models (DEMs).
38.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

39.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

40.definition: Defined areas for the purposes of oil and mineral exploitation
40.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

41.definition: External datasets sourced from other Agencies
41.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

42.definition: This includes for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams and field mapping survey and site information including field geology observations and measurements.
42.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

43.definition: Measurements taken at a field site such as structural, spectral (PIMA) or geophysical (magnetic susceptibility)
43.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

44.definition: Visual observations and photos of the vegetation, landscape and geomorphology at a field site and descriptions of the mineralogy, lithology, alteration of rocks, samples and outcrops at a field site.
44.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

45.definition: Hydraulic conductivity, porosity, permeability, specific discharge
45.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

46.definition: Data related to the chemical analyses and composition of earth materials. This includes information related to the chemical analysis of organic material, surficial, groundwater and pore water samples, benthic chemical analysis, and stable isotope studies
46.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

47.definition: Age approximations of earth materials such as rocks, sediments and minerals. Age data resulting from scientific techniques such as radio isotopes and chemical dating.
47.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

48.definition: Measurements and representation of the earth, its gravitational field and geodynamic phenomena (polar motion, earth tides, and crustal motion) in three-dimensional time varying space.
48.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

49.definition: The study of the origin of landscapes based on a cause and effect relationship. This includes the physical and chemical interactions between the earth's surface and the natural forces acting on it, such as geology, climate, vegetation and relative base level.
49.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

50.definition: Includes seismic, gravity and airborne survey locations. For example, seismic navigation data.
50.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

51.definition: Interpretation using geophysical techniques and data
51.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

52.definition: Data of the physical characteristics of the earth (crust, mantle, core)  such as gravity, magnetics, radiometrics, and seismic. This also includes information relating to basement and crustal studies.
52.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

53.definition: A Geothermal hole is drilled specifically for the exploration, development, appraisal or monitoring of geothermal plays or resources
53.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

54.definition: Including ground, airborne, point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images
54.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

55.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

56.definition: Natural  and human environmental phenomena, whether slow or fast acting.  Information on the natural environment and its interaction with geology, eg waste disposal and pollution such as geological disposal of CO2.
56.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

57.definition: Includes groundwater, porewater and surface water
57.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

58.definition: Hydroacoustic sensing involves "passive acoustics" (listening for sounds) or active acoustics making a sound and listening for the echo, hence the common name for the device, echo sounder or echosounder.
58.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

59.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

60.definition: The distribution and movement of groundwater and dissolved materials in the soil and rocks of the Earth's crust (commonly in aquifers).
60.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

61.definition: Information related to the distribution,  mapping and physical characteristics of surficial waters. Examples include the locations of bores, lakes, dams, canals, sea levels etc,
61.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

62.definition: Infrasound is sound that is lower in frequency than 20 cycles per second, the normal limit of human hearing. The study of such sound waves is sometimes referred to as infrasonics, covering sounds beneath 20 Hz down to 0.001 Hz. This frequency range is utilized for monitoring earthquakes, charting rock and petroleum formations below the earth
62.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

63.definition: Any structure fixed to a terrestrial or marine location to facilitate commercial or daily activities, or the supply of essential services. Examples include processing plants / factories, ports, oil rigs etc. This includes information regarding the facilitation of transporting  people or commodities.
63.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

64.definition: Describing a substance not derived from a living organism and/or not composed of carbon and hydrogen (a hydrocarbon).
64.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

65.definition: Information related to the interpretation of  baseline geoscientific information integrated with field and petrographic observations and measurements. Examples include the interpretation of geological provinces, outcrop and basement geology, 3D geology models, geophysical imagery stratigraphic cross-sections.
65.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

66.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

67.definition: LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is an optical remote sensing technology that measures properties of scattered light to find range and/or other information of a distant target. The prevalent method to determine distance to an object or surface is to use laser pulses. Like the similar radar technology, which uses radio waves instead of light, the range to an object is determined by measuring the time delay between transmission of a pulse and detection of the reflected signal.
67.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

68.definition: Magnetic Susceptibility is the degree of magnetization of a material in response to an applied magnetic field.
68.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

69.definition: Point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images
69.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

70.definition: Manufacturing is the transformation, by means of a tool and/or processing medium, of raw materials into finished goods for sale, or intermediate processes involving the production or finishing of semi-manufactures. It is a large branch of industry and of secondary production.
70.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

71.definition: Zones of similar mineralogy caused by heat and pressure
71.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

72.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

73.definition: Includes mineral occurrences, resources, deposits, mineral potential, mineral deposit models, prospectivity maps
73.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

74.definition: A Mineral hole is drilled for the exploration, development or resource definition of mineral commodities
74.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

75.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

76.definition: The distribution of plants, animals; natural biotic distribution patterns and habitats for aerial, terrestrial, marine, and other life.BIOME: are distinct ecological communities of plants and animals living together in a particular climate. Scientists classify biomes in various ways, but the major biomes include aquatic, deserts, forests, grasslands, and tundra.?
76.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

77.definition: Optical remote sensing makes use of visible, near infrared and short-waveinfrared sensors to form images of the earth's surface by detecting thesolar radiation reflected from targets on the ground. Different materials reflect and absorb differently at different wavelengths. Thus, the targets can be differentiated by their spectral reflectance signatures in the remotely sensed images. Optical remote sensing systems are classified into the following types, depending on the number of spectral bands used in the imaging process.
77.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

78.definition: Material  derived from living organisms and is made up of carbon-based compounds.
78.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

79.definition: Interpretation of the nature and distribution of outcropping (or near-outcropping) rock units and structures, typically in the form of maps.  These maps may include integrated regolith unit information in areas of non-outcropping bedrock.
79.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

80.definition: The climate of a given interval of time in the the geologic past.
80.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

81.definition: An environment in the geologic past
81.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

82.definition: Palinspastic reconstructions or geological time-slice interpretations
82.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

83.definition: The study of life in past geologic time, based on fossil plants and animals. Chronology of the Earth's history.
83.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

84.definition: Passive geophysical surveys are ones that incorporate measurements of naturally occurring fields or properties of the earth.
84.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

85.definition: Petrographic observations and measurements such as mineralogy, lithology, alteration, mineralisation, metamorphic grade, weathering and grainsize in specimens and thin sections (micro scale).
85.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

86.definition: A Petroleum Well is drilled for the exploration, development or appraisal of hydrocarbons (oil or gas)
86.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

87.definition: Includes thin section, fluid inclusion observations and measurements of hand specimens from the surface or down boreholes
87.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

88.definition: Data related to the physical expression of the earths surface either terrestrial or marine. Examples include marine bathymetric information, seafloor expressions (mounts, slopes, shelf, rise) freshwater/estuarine bed profiles and  Digital Elevation Models
88.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

89.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

90.definition: Porosity and Permeability are two of the primary factors that control the movement and storage of fluids in rocks and sediments. They are intrinsic characteristics of these geologic materials. The exploitation of natural resources, such as groundwater and petroleum, is partly dependent on the properties of porosity and permeability.
90.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

91.definition: Descriptions and polygon outlines of geological provinces of the Australian continent and surrounding offshore regions.
91.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

92.definition: RADAR stands for "RAdio Detection And Ranging". By virtue of sending out pulses of microwave electromagnetic radiation this type of instrument can be classified as an "active sensor" - it measures the time between pulses and their reflected components to determine distance. Different pulse intervals, different wavelengths, different geometry and polarizations can be combined to roughness characteristics of the earth surface.
92.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

93.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

94.definition: Point located data, grids, derivative grids (filtered, etc), algorithms, images
94.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

95.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

96.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

97.definition: Legal boundaries like mining tenements and cadastre
97.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

98.definition: Data on resource production and/or commodity potential or information. Examples include minerals and petroleum potential, oil and gas fields information, resource and production tonnage and grade information, deposit types and mineralisation styles
98.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

99.definition: Physical measurements of rock properties such as density and susceptibility for use in predictive modelling studies such as seismic predictions, geophysical inversions, and groundwater distribution, or engineering work.
99.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

100.definition: Contains the sampling locations of features which were the target for a set of observations and/or measurements.
100.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

101.definition: SEGY, seismic profiles, images, processed data, weak and strong motion from seismographs and accelerators
101.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

102.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

103.definition: Environmental events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as salinity, acid-sulphate soils, coastal erosion, desertification, climate change
103.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

104.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

105.definition: An Anthropogenic feature to facilitate cultural, societal or religious activities eg cemeteries, recreational areas, land uses related to primary industries,  building infrastructure etc. Excludes information relating to transportation.
105.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

106.definition: Similar data to other geophysical types, but in the "spectral geology" space, with many purposes. For example, PIMA and Hylogger
106.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

107.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

108.definition: A Stratigraphic hole is drilled to obtain a record of the Stratigraphic character, composition or property of the rock/soil/regolith units penetrated
108.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

109.definition: Sudden events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as earthquake, landslide, flood, tsunami, wildfire, tropical cyclone, storm surge, severe windstorm
109.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

110.definition: Examples include potentiometric and phreatic surfaces
110.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

111.definition: Information regarding the location, purpose and methodology related to particular surveys of any kind. This includes, for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams. This category can be summarised as metadata regarding any type of survey undertaken or managed within the Organisation.
111.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

112.definition: The theory and practice of classifying plants and animals.
112.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

113.definition: For example, land vegetation - pest and native flora and fauna
113.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

114.definition: Sudden emergency events associated with acts of terrorism causing damage to human life or the economy
114.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

115.definition: Geological datasets developed for a specific purpose, such as "Volcanoes of the World"
115.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

116.definition: Remote sensing of direct temperature effects is carried out by sensing radiation emitted from matter in the thermal infrared region of the spectrum.
116.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

117.definition: Thermal properties of fluids, gases and solids
117.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

118.definition: The shapes, patterns and physical configuration of the surface of the land, including its relief (local differences in elevation) and the positions of natural and man-made features.
118.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

119.definition: Data relating to any infrastructure facilitating the movement of people or goods by motorised means. This includes roads, railways and flight paths, aerodromes etc.
119.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

120.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

121.definition: Information relating to the supply or provision of utility services such as electricity, gas, steam, water or sewage. Examples include oil , gas , electricity and water pipelines.
121.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

122.definition: A Water Bore is drilled specifically for the exploration, extraction or monitoring of groundwater resources
122.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

123.definition: Reflected waves can be generated from a planar boundary in seismic wave speed that can be recorded at the Earth's surface
123.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

126.definition: The recorded date is the earliest known date, but it is possible the true date is earlier than what is recorded.

127.definition: The recorded date is the latest known date, but it is possible the true date is later than what is recorded.

129.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:1 000 000 scale TEST

130.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:100 000 scale

131.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:25 000 scale

132.definition: Location estimated from an orthophoto at 1:25 000 scale

133.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:250 000 scale

134.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:50 000 scale

135.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:500 000 scale

136.definition: Location estimated from an aerial photograph that has not been rectified to a known geographical datum or projection

137.definition: Location is estimated from an astronomical observation

138.prefLabel: astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes
138.definition: Location is estimated from an astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes.

139.definition: The location is supplied by a private company. The location method is not known.

140.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation

141.prefLabel: dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes
141.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes

142.prefLabel: dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes and differential GPS
142.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes and differential GPS

143.prefLabel: dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes and stand-alone GPS
143.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes and stand alone GPS

144.definition: Location estimated from a digital elevation model or image

145.definition: Location estimated from a 9 second resolution digital elevation model or image

146.definition: Location estimated from a 90 metre resolution digital elevation model or image of data from the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission

147.definition: Location estimated from averaging a number of Global Positioning System observations

148.definition: Location estimated using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.

149.definition: Location determined using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.

150.definition: Location estimated from a Global Positioning System satellite navigation system

151.prefLabel: GPS observation subject to union all selective availability
151.definition: Location estimated from a Global Positioning System prior to May 2000 before when the US government commonly degraded the accuracy of GPS signals by adding intentional, time varying errors of up to 100 meters to the publicly available navigation signal.

152.definition: Location estimated from GPS observation with P-Code access

153.definition: Location estimated from a printed topographic, geological, or other map

154.definition: Location estimated from an aerial photograph geometrically corrected ("orthorectified") such that the scale across the image is uniform

155.definition: The location method is known, but is not specified in this vocabulary

156.definition: The location is supplied via personal communication.  The location method is not known.

157.definition: The location is taken from a published report or article.  The location method is not known.

159.prefLabel: satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation
159.definition: Location estimated from satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation

160.definition: Location estimated from a satellite image

161.definition: Location estimated from a satellite image provided by Google Earth or Google Maps

162.prefLabel: State or NT agency supplied location, method unknown
162.definition: The location is supplied by a State or Territory government agency. The location method is not known.

163.definition: Location estimated using survey from ground control

164.definition: The location method is unknown. However, the correct value probably exists.

165.definition: The location is taken from an unpublished report.  The location method is not known.

Location estimated from a Global Positioning System prior to May 2000 before when the US government commonly degraded the accuracy of GPS signals by adding intentional, time varying errors of up to 100 meters to the publicly available navigation signal.
Location estimated from a Global Positioning System satellite navigation system
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:1 000 000 scale TEST
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:100 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:25 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:250 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:50 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:500 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed topographic, geological, or other map
Location estimated from a satellite image
Location estimated from a satellite image provided by Google Earth or Google Maps
Location estimated from an aerial photograph geometrically corrected ("orthorectified") such that the scale across the image is uniform
Location estimated from an aerial photograph that has not been rectified to a known geographical datum or projection
Location estimated from an orthophoto at 1:25 000 scale
Location estimated from averaging a number of Global Positioning System observations
Location estimated from GPS observation with P-Code access
Location estimated from satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation

Location estimated using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.
Location estimated using survey from ground control
Location is estimated from an astronomical observation
Location is estimated from an astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes.
Magnetic Susceptibility is the degree of magnetization of a material in response to an applied magnetic field.

Manufacturing is the transformation, by means of a tool and/or processing medium, of raw materials into finished goods for sale, or intermediate processes involving the production or finishing of semi-manufactures. It is a large branch of industry and of secondary production.
Material  derived from living organisms and is made up of carbon-based compounds.
Measurements and representation of the earth, its gravitational field and geodynamic phenomena (polar motion, earth tides, and crustal motion) in three-dimensional time varying space.
Measurements of ocean depth and the charting of the topography of the ocean floor.
Measurements taken at a field site such as structural, spectral (PIMA) or geophysical (magnetic susceptibility)

Natural  and human environmental phenomena, whether slow or fast acting.  Information on the natural environment and its interaction with geology, eg waste disposal and pollution such as geological disposal of CO2.

Optical remote sensing makes use of visible, near infrared and short-waveinfrared sensors to form images of the earth's surface by detecting thesolar radiation reflected from targets on the ground. Different materials reflect and absorb differently at different wavelengths. Thus, the targets can be differentiated by their spectral reflectance signatures in the remotely sensed images. Optical remote sensing systems are classified into the following types, depending on the number of spectral bands used in the imaging process.
Palinspastic reconstructions or geological time-slice interpretations
part of a set of imagery that when used together, provides three-dimensional images
part of same structured set of data held in a computer
Passive geophysical surveys are ones that incorporate measurements of naturally occurring fields or properties of the earth.
Pertains to both marine and freshwater ecosystems. For example, reefs
Petrographic observations and measurements such as mineralogy, lithology, alteration, mineralisation, metamorphic grade, weathering and grainsize in specimens and thin sections (micro scale).

Photographs of a part of the earth's surface taken by a camera mounted in an aircraft for mapping purposes. This usually consists of a series of overlapping vertical photos taken in strips which can form the basis for mapping.

Physical measurements of rock properties such as density and susceptibility for use in predictive modelling studies such as seismic predictions, geophysical inversions, and groundwater distribution, or engineering work.
Point located data, grids, derivative grids (filtered, etc), algorithms, images
Point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images

Porosity and Permeability are two of the primary factors that control the movement and storage of fluids in rocks and sediments. They are intrinsic characteristics of these geologic materials. The exploitation of natural resources, such as groundwater and petroleum, is partly dependent on the properties of porosity and permeability.

RADAR stands for "RAdio Detection And Ranging". By virtue of sending out pulses of microwave electromagnetic radiation this type of instrument can be classified as an "active sensor" - it measures the time between pulses and their reflected components to determine distance. Different pulse intervals, different wavelengths, different geometry and polarizations can be combined to roughness characteristics of the earth surface.
reference from one resource to another - TEST
reference to a master resource of which this one is a part
reference to resources that are parts of this resource
Reflected waves can be generated from a planar boundary in seismic wave speed that can be recorded at the Earth's surface
Remote sensing of direct temperature effects is carried out by sensing radiation emitted from matter in the thermal infrared region of the spectrum.
resource is a revision of associated resource

Satellite and air platform imagery. This includes imagery designed to represent surficial condition of the earth such as Landsat, SPOT, MODIS, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography. Remotely sensed data acquired from earth observation satellites and Sensing Instruments for both scientific and operational applications, such as LANDSAT 5,LANDSAT 7,RADARSAT 1 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR), ERS 2 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR) TERRA Modis,AQUA Modis, NOAA 17, SPOT, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography
SEGY, seismic profiles, images, processed data, weak and strong motion from seismographs and accelerators
Similar data to other geophysical types, but in the "spectral geology" space, with many purposes. For example, PIMA and Hylogger
Stratigraphy based on the paleontologic aspects of rocks.
Sudden emergency events associated with acts of terrorism causing damage to human life or the economy
Sudden events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as earthquake, landslide, flood, tsunami, wildfire, tropical cyclone, storm surge, severe windstorm
The benthic zone is the ecological region at the lowest level of a body of water such as an ocean or a lake, including the sediment surface and some sub-surface layers.
The climate of a given interval of time in the the geologic past.
The density of a material is defined as its mass per unit volume.
The distribution and movement of groundwater and dissolved materials in the soil and rocks of the Earth's crust (commonly in aquifers).

The distribution of plants, animals; natural biotic distribution patterns and habitats for aerial, terrestrial, marine, and other life.BIOME: are distinct ecological communities of plants and animals living together in a particular climate. Scientists classify biomes in various ways, but the major biomes include aquatic, deserts, forests, grasslands, and tundra.?
the domain resource was derived from the range resource which must be an entity (i.e. not an actor with agency or a temporal event)
the domain resource was generated by the range resource which must have been a temporal event such as a data processing action or a field survey

The domain resource was informed by the range resource which must have been an Entity (i.e. not an Agent with agency or an Activity temporal event). This is a looser association than wasDerivedFrom and does not indicate direct derivation, as wasDerivedFrom does.
The location is supplied by a private company. The location method is not known.
The location is supplied by a State or Territory government agency. The location method is not known.
The location is supplied via personal communication.  The location method is not known.
The location is taken from a published report or article.  The location method is not known.
The location is taken from an unpublished report.  The location method is not known.
The location method is known, but is not specified in this vocabulary
The location method is unknown. However, the correct value probably exists.
The recorded date is the earliest known date, but it is possible the true date is earlier than what is recorded.
The recorded date is the latest known date, but it is possible the true date is later than what is recorded.
The shapes, patterns and physical configuration of the surface of the land, including its relief (local differences in elevation) and the positions of natural and man-made features.
The study of life in past geologic time, based on fossil plants and animals. Chronology of the Earth's history.

The study of the origin of landscapes based on a cause and effect relationship. This includes the physical and chemical interactions between the earth's surface and the natural forces acting on it, such as geology, climate, vegetation and relative base level.
The theory and practice of classifying plants and animals.
The use of non-radiogenic dating studies such as U-Pb ratios
Thermal properties of fluids, gases and solids
This includes for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams and field mapping survey and site information including field geology observations and measurements.
Visual observations and photos of the vegetation, landscape and geomorphology at a field site and descriptions of the mineralogy, lithology, alteration of rocks, samples and outcrops at a field site.
Zones of similar mineralogy caused by heat and pressure
